serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Zi Char salted fish chicken fried rice weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-person serving at a hawker centre or Zi Char restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore's published nutrient composition data for fried rice dishes and component ingredients (cooked white rice, chicken, salted fish, egg). The input values (energy 173 kcal, protein 8.4 g, fat 7.8 g, carbohydrate 18.9 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB reference ranges for chicken fried rice variants and have been retained. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g reflects the salted fish component and is consistent with HPB data for similar dishes. Values for monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database for this dish category and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted fish chicken fried rice. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice fried rice (GI ~72, glucose reference), which is the closest analogous food. The dish is predominantly white rice (high-GI refined carbohydrate). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 84.99) / 100 ≈ 61.1 for a 450 g plate (18.89 g carb/100 g × 4.5 = 85.0 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400 mg/100 g, primarily from salted fish and soy sauce, with a 450 g serving delivering approximately 1800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load from salted fish; if consumed, portion should be restricted to no more than 150 g and low-sodium soy sauce substituted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from white rice base with low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g), resulting in rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation and a high glycemic load of ~61 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice, increasing vegetable content, and limiting portion to 150–200 g can reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (400 mg/100 g), moderate cholesterol (55.56 mg/100 g), and saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g) alongside a refined carbohydrate base with minimal fibre, contributing to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly; the high sodium from salted fish poses the greatest risk and should be reduced by omitting or minimising the salted fish component and using heart-healthy cooking oils.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salted fish is a highly processed, high-sodium ingredient that exacerbates RA-related tissue inflammation and fluid retention; the refined white rice base contributes to glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should reduce or omit salted fish, substitute white rice with brown rice, and consider adding anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or turmeric to the stir-fry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Rice with Diced Chicken and Salted Fish (咸鱼鸡粒炒饭, Xian Yu Ji Li Chao Fan) is a quintessential Singaporean Zi Char dish prepared by wok-frying cooked white rice at high heat with bite-sized diced chicken, small pieces of preserved salted fish, beaten egg, spring onion, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The salted fish — typically threadfin (马友鱼, ma you yu) or yellow croaker — is the defining flavour element, imparting an intense, pungent umami depth that distinguishes this dish from plain chicken fried rice. The wok hei (breath of the wok) technique produces a lightly charred, fragrant aroma. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 173 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 18.9 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400 mg — a significant contribution from the salted fish and soy sauce. Dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g/100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 72 (high), making this dish a high-glycemic-load meal when consumed in a standard 450 g plate serving (GL ≈ 61). Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (very high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high GI, low fibre), or cardiovascular disease (high sodium, moderate saturated fat, refined carbohydrate). It is a popular hawker centre and Zi Char restaurant staple across Singapore and Malaysia, often ordered as a shared dish or individual plate meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore's published data for fried rice dishes and are consistent with the input record sourced from GrabFood. The core macros are plausible and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice fried rice, not a direct measurement for this specific dish. Several micronutrient fields (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, B-vitamins, vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this dish category and are set to -1.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Zi Char fried rice preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this dish as of 2024. 2. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Malaysian' and included 'Crab' in ethnic_tags — these appear to be data entry errors; this dish is a Singaporean Chinese Zi Char preparation and contains no crab; corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese (Zi Char)' and ethnic_tags updated accordingly. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100 g from the input source; this is a trace level consistent with small amounts from cooking oils and is retained. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice fried rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for salted fish chicken fried rice was found in approved sources. 5. Halal status is marked false as salted fish fried rice from Zi Char stalls may use non-halal ingredients or be prepared in non-halal kitchens; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific vendor.
